enrichment.short_description: Semgrep is a fast, open-source static application security testing (SAST) tool that scans source code for bugs, vulnerabilities, and secrets using lightweight, pattern-based rules across 30-plus programming languages.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free open-source scanner plus paid Team and Enterprise tiers)
enrichment.starting_price: Free for up to 10 repositories and 10 contributors; paid Team plan starts at $30 per contributor per month
enrichment.seo_title: Semgrep Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Semgrep costs in 2026, how its static analysis engine works, and whether the Free, Team, or Enterprise plan fits your AppSec workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Semgrep is an open-source static application security testing (SAST) tool created by the company Semgrep (formerly r2c), which was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Semgrep scans source code for bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code quality issues using rules that are written in a syntax resembling the target programming language itself, rather than a complex abstract-syntax-tree query language, which makes custom rule authoring accessible to developers who are not security specialists. The platform supports more than 30 programming languages at varying maturity levels, with Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, C and C++, Ruby, PHP, Rust, C#, and Kotlin generally available. Semgrep is distributed as three product lines: Semgrep Code for static analysis with cross-file and cross-function data-flow tracing and access to more than 20,000 Pro rules maintained by Semgrep's security research team; Semgrep Supply Chain for software composition analysis that uses reachability analysis to flag only the vulnerable open-source dependencies actually called by application code, reducing noise from irrelevant CVEs; and Semgrep Secrets for detecting hardcoded credentials and API keys with validation checks. The product also includes AI-assisted triage and autofix suggestions, consumed through an AI-credit allowance that scales by plan. Pricing follows three tiers. The Free (Community) edition costs nothing and covers up to 10 repositories, up to 10 contributors, 60 AI credits, and both the Code and Supply Chain products with cross-file analysis using Pro rules. The Team plan starts at $30 per contributor per month, covering up to 500 private repositories, a choice of one core product (with the Secrets add-on priced separately at $15 per month), 20 AI credits per developer per month, single sign-on via OIDC and SAML, REST API access, and distributed scanning. The Enterprise tier uses custom, volume-based pricing and adds unlimited repositories and contributors, 50 AI credits per developer per month, support for on-premise source-code management systems, custom CI/CD integrations, a dedicated account manager, and custom AI model provider support. Semgrep integrates directly into developer workflows through GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, and IDE plugins such as VS Code, allowing scans to run automatically in pull requests or pre-commit hooks. It competes with tools such as Snyk, CodeQL, SonarQube, and Checkmarx in the DevSecOps and application security space, and is commonly adopted by engineering and security teams that want fast, low-noise static analysis embedded directly into CI/CD pipelines rather than a separate, slow security review stage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Semgrep is a static application security testing tool built to find bugs, vulnerabilities, and hardcoded secrets directly inside source code before it reaches production. Founded in 2017 and headquartered in San Francisco, the company built Semgrep around a simple idea: security rules should look like the code they are scanning, so developers, not just security specialists, can write and maintain them.

The platform now spans three connected products, Semgrep Code for SAST, Semgrep Supply Chain for dependency risk, and Semgrep Secrets for credential leaks, all sharing a single scanning engine, rule registry, and dashboard so security and engineering teams can manage findings from one place.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Semgrep's core differentiator is cross-file and cross-function analysis that traces how data flows through an application, which lets it catch multi-step vulnerabilities that simple single-file pattern matching would miss. It ships with more than 20,000 Pro rules curated by Semgrep's research team, on top of a public rule registry contributed to by the open-source community.

Semgrep Supply Chain applies reachability analysis to open-source dependency scanning, surfacing only the vulnerable packages that are actually invoked by application code, which meaningfully cuts down on the alert fatigue common with traditional CVE scanners. AI-powered triage and autofix features, gated behind a per-plan AI-credit allowance, help teams prioritize and resolve findings faster.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Semgrep offers a free Community edition covering up to 10 repositories and 10 contributors with both Code and Supply Chain included. The Team plan starts at $30 per contributor per month for up to 500 private repositories, with the Secrets product available as a $15 per month add-on.

Enterprise pricing is custom and scales with the number of developers, deployment model (cloud, self-hosted, or hybrid), and required features, and typically involves a direct sales conversation for large or regulated organizations that need on-premise source-code management support.

features.title: Cross-file and cross-function analysis
features.description: Traces data flow across multiple files and functions to catch complex, multi-step vulnerabilities that single-file pattern matching would miss.

features.title: Semgrep Code (SAST)
features.description: Over 20,000 Pro rules maintained by Semgrep's security research team, plus community rules from the public Semgrep Registry.

features.title: Semgrep Supply Chain (SCA)
features.description: Reachability-based dependency scanning that flags only vulnerable open-source packages actually called by application code.

features.title: Semgrep Secrets
features.description: Detects hardcoded credentials and API keys with validation checks to reduce false positives.

features.title: AI-powered triage and autofix
features.description: Uses an AI-credit allowance to suggest fixes and auto-triage findings, cutting down manual review time.

features.title: CI/CD and IDE integrations
features.description: Plugs into GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, and editors like VS Code to run scans automatically in pull requests or pre-commit hooks.

features.title: Code-like custom rule authoring
features.description: Custom rules are written in a syntax that resembles the target source code rather than a complex AST query language.

features.title: Flexible deployment for Enterprise
features.description: Self-hosted, cloud, or hybrid deployment with support for on-premise source-code management systems.

pros: Free and open-source core scanner with a genuinely usable free tier for small teams
Fast, lightweight scanning that fits naturally into CI/CD pipelines
Custom rules use familiar code-like syntax instead of a complex query language
Reachability analysis in Supply Chain reduces alert fatigue from irrelevant dependency CVEs
Broad language coverage across 30-plus languages suits polyglot codebases
cons: Team and Enterprise pricing is per-contributor, which can get expensive for large engineering organizations
Secrets detection is a separate paid add-on rather than bundled into the base Team plan
Enterprise pricing is custom and opaque, requiring a sales conversation for larger deployments
Some advanced Pro rules and cross-file analysis depth are limited on the free tier
Like most SAST tools, tuning is needed to minimize false positives on large legacy codebases

faqs.question: What is Semgrep used for?
faqs.answer: Semgrep is used for static application security testing, scanning source code to find bugs, security vulnerabilities, vulnerable dependencies, and hardcoded secrets before code reaches production.

faqs.question: Is Semgrep free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Semgrep has a free Community edition covering up to 10 repositories and 10 contributors with Code and Supply Chain scanning included. Larger teams need the paid Team or Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Semgrep Code, Supply Chain, and Secrets?
faqs.answer: Semgrep Code is the SAST engine for finding bugs and vulnerabilities in first-party code, Supply Chain is software composition analysis for open-source dependency risk, and Secrets detects hardcoded credentials and API keys.

faqs.question: How much does Semgrep Team cost?
faqs.answer: The Team plan starts at $30 per contributor per month for up to 500 private repositories, with the Secrets product available as a $15 per month add-on.

faqs.question: What programming languages does Semgrep support?
faqs.answer: Semgrep supports more than 30 languages, with Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, C, C++, Ruby, PHP, Rust, C#, and Kotlin at general availability.

faqs.question: Who founded Semgrep and when?
faqs.answer: Semgrep was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Can Semgrep be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Enterprise customers can deploy Semgrep in the cloud, self-hosted, or in a hybrid model, including support for on-premise source-code management systems.

faqs.question: Does Semgrep integrate with CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes, Semgrep integrates with GitHub, GitLab, Jenkins, and IDEs such as VS Code so scans can run automatically on pull requests or before commits.

use_cases.title: DevSecOps pipeline scanning
use_cases.description: Security and platform engineering teams embed Semgrep directly into CI/CD pipelines to catch vulnerabilities and secrets before merge.

use_cases.title: Custom internal rule authoring
use_cases.description: Security engineers write custom Semgrep rules to enforce internal framework or API usage patterns unique to their codebase.

use_cases.title: Open-source and pre-commit scanning
use_cases.description: Individual developers and open-source maintainers run the free Semgrep scanner locally or as a pre-commit hook to catch issues early.

features: Semgrep Code and Supply Chain products included
Choice of Code (SAST) or Supply Chain (SCA); Secrets available for $15 per month
20 AI credits per developer per month
Single sign-on (SSO) with OIDC and SAML
50 AI credits per developer per month

enrichment.short_description: Semrush is an all-in-one digital marketing platform offering keyword research, technical site audits, competitor analysis, backlink tracking, PPC research, content marketing, and AI-driven search visibility tools used by over 1.8 million marketers worldwide.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription, billed monthly or annually
enrichment.starting_price: $139.95 per month for the Pro plan billed monthly, or about $117.33 per month billed annually
enrichment.company_size: 1,500-plus employees; publicly traded on the NYSE under the ticker SEMR
enrichment.seo_title: Semrush Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Plans Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Semrush pricing plans, core SEO and PPC features, and company background in 2026 to decide if the platform fits your marketing stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Semrush is a publicly traded, all-in-one digital marketing platform founded in 2008 and headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, with additional offices in Pennsylvania, Texas, and the Czech Republic. Trading on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SEMR, the company employs more than 1,500 people and reports annual revenue exceeding $330 million, serving over 1.8 million marketing professionals across 142 countries, including a large share of Fortune 500 companies. What began as a single SEO keyword tool has grown into a suite of 55-plus individual tools spanning organic search, paid advertising, content marketing, social media management, local SEO, competitive intelligence, and newer AI-driven generative engine optimization (GEO) features that track brand visibility inside AI chat and answer engines. Core capabilities include the Keyword Magic Tool for keyword research across a database covering billions of keywords in 142-plus country-level databases, Site Audit for technical SEO health checks covering crawlability, indexability, and Core Web Vitals, Position Tracking for daily rank monitoring by location and device, Backlink Analytics and Backlink Audit for link-profile analysis and toxic-link identification, On Page SEO Checker and SEO Writing Assistant for content optimization, and Advertising Research and PPC Keyword Tool for competitor ad-spend and paid-search intelligence. Semrush's interface is localized into ten languages, including English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Turkish, Vietnamese, Chinese, and Japanese, reflecting its global user base. Pricing is structured in three core self-serve tiers: Pro at $139.95 per month (about $117.33 per month billed annually) aimed at freelancers and small teams with 5 projects and 500 tracked keywords; Guru at $249.95 per month (about $208.33 annually) adding historical data, the content marketing toolkit, and Looker Studio integration with 15 projects and 1,500 tracked keywords; and Business at $499.95 per month (about $416.66 annually) adding API access and extended limits across 40 projects and 5,000 tracked keywords. Semrush also offers a separate Semrush One bundle starting around $199 per month with Starter, Pro+, and Advanced tiers. The platform competes directly with Ahrefs, Moz, and Ubersuggest in the SEO tooling category, and its scope has expanded well beyond SEO into a broader marketing operations suite, making it one of the most recognizable brands in search marketing software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Semrush was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. The company went public and now trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker SEMR, employing more than 1,500 people worldwide and serving over 1.8 million marketing professionals across 142 countries.

Originally built around keyword research, Semrush has expanded into a full digital marketing suite of 55-plus tools covering SEO, PPC, content marketing, social media, local search, and AI search visibility, positioning itself as a single platform for most organic and paid marketing workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Semrush's core SEO toolkit includes the Keyword Magic Tool for keyword research, Site Audit for technical SEO health checks including Core Web Vitals, Position Tracking for rank monitoring by location and device, and Backlink Analytics for link-profile and toxic-link analysis.

Beyond core SEO, the platform includes Advertising Research and a PPC Keyword Tool for competitor ad intelligence, a Content Marketing toolkit and SEO Writing Assistant for optimizing content, a social media scheduling tool, and newer generative engine optimization features that track how brands appear in AI-generated answers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Semrush offers three primary self-serve subscription tiers: Pro at $139.95 per month, Guru at $249.95 per month, and Business at $499.95 per month, each with a discount of roughly 15 to 17 percent when billed annually.

Higher tiers unlock more projects, more tracked keywords, historical data, API access, and extended reporting limits, and Semrush also sells a separate Semrush One bundle with its own Starter, Pro+, and Advanced pricing tiers starting around $199 per month.

faqs.question: What is Semrush used for?
faqs.answer: Semrush is used for SEO keyword research, technical site audits, rank tracking, backlink analysis, PPC competitor research, and broader digital marketing tasks like content and social media planning.

faqs.question: How much does Semrush cost?
faqs.answer: Semrush's core plans start at $139.95 per month for Pro, $249.95 per month for Guru, and $499.95 per month for Business, with lower rates available on annual billing.

faqs.question: Is Semrush publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Yes, Semrush is a publicly traded company on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol SEMR.

faqs.question: Does Semrush offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Semrush commonly offers limited free trials or a restricted free account for basic lookups, though full functionality requires a paid subscription.

faqs.question: What languages does the Semrush interface support?
faqs.answer: The Semrush interface is available in English, Spanish, German, French, Italian, Portuguese, Turkish, Vietnamese, Chinese, and Japanese.

faqs.question: Where is Semrush headquartered?
faqs.answer: Semrush is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, with additional offices including locations in Pennsylvania, Texas, and the Czech Republic.

faqs.question: Does Semrush have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, API access is available, primarily on the higher-tier Business plan.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Semrush?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Ahrefs, Moz Pro, and Ubersuggest, each offering overlapping but differently scoped SEO and marketing research tools.

enrichment.short_description: Sender is an email and SMS marketing platform built for small businesses and ecommerce brands, offering a free-forever plan, a drag-and-drop email builder, marketing automation, and transactional email delivery.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription plus a pay-as-you-go credit option
enrichment.starting_price: Free forever for up to 2,500 subscribers and 15,000 emails per month; paid plans start around $7 per month
enrichment.company_size: Around 30 employees; bootstrapped with no outside venture funding
enrichment.seo_title: Sender Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Free Plan Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Sender's free-forever plan, paid pricing tiers, and email and SMS marketing features for small businesses in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sender is an email and SMS marketing platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in Vilnius, Lithuania, run as a bootstrapped company with no outside venture funding and a team of roughly 30 employees. The company is led by CEO Edgaras Vaitkevičius and positions itself as an affordable alternative to larger email service providers like Mailchimp, aiming its free-forever plan and low-cost paid tiers primarily at small businesses, solo creators, and ecommerce brands with limited marketing budgets. The free-forever plan is notably generous for a tool in this category, covering up to 2,500 subscribers and 15,000 emails per month while unlocking nearly all core platform features, including automation, transactional emails, and unlimited campaigns and seats, rather than gating them behind a paywall as many competitors do. Paid plans begin around $7 per month for the Standard tier with three role-based seats, scaling to about $14 per month for the Professional tier with ten seats, with exact pricing determined by subscriber list size. Sender also offers a pay-as-you-go credit option that lets customers purchase sending credits without committing to a recurring monthly subscription, which suits businesses with irregular or seasonal sending volume. Core features include a drag-and-drop email builder with pre-built templates, marketing automation workflows and autoresponders, transactional and triggered emails sent via SMTP or API, SMS marketing delivered alongside email campaigns from the same dashboard, pop-ups and signup forms for growing subscriber lists, and audience segmentation with basic analytics and reporting. Combining email and SMS within a single affordable platform is one of Sender's key differentiators from single-channel competitors. Because it is a smaller, bootstrapped company, Sender's third-party integration ecosystem and advanced reporting capabilities are less extensive than those of larger, well-funded email service providers such as Mailchimp or Klaviyo, and its deliverability track record and brand recognition are less established given its smaller scale and shorter operating history in the mainstream ESP market. Even so, its combination of a free tier with nearly full feature access, unlimited seats and campaigns on paid plans, and simple, transparent per-subscriber pricing makes it a commonly recommended option for budget-conscious small businesses and newsletter publishers evaluating entry-level email marketing software in 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sender is a bootstrapped email and SMS marketing platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in Vilnius, Lithuania. The company has grown without outside venture funding and employs roughly 30 people, positioning itself as an affordable, feature-rich alternative to larger email service providers.

Sender targets small businesses, solo creators, and ecommerce brands, with its free-forever plan and low-cost paid tiers designed to unlock most core functionality, including automation and transactional email, without requiring an upgrade for basic use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sender combines a drag-and-drop email builder, marketing automation workflows, transactional and triggered emails via SMTP or API, and SMS marketing within a single dashboard, letting small teams manage multiple channels without separate tools.

Additional features include pop-ups and signup forms for list growth, audience segmentation, basic analytics and reporting, and a pay-as-you-go credit option for businesses that prefer not to commit to a recurring subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sender's free-forever plan covers up to 2,500 subscribers and 15,000 emails per month with nearly all core features unlocked, which is more generous than many competing free tiers.

Paid plans start around $7 per month for the Standard tier and about $14 per month for the Professional tier, both scaling with subscriber list size, with unlimited seats and campaigns included, and a pay-as-you-go credit option available as an alternative to a recurring subscription.

enrichment.short_description: SendGrid, now branded Twilio SendGrid, is a cloud-based transactional and marketing email platform offering an Email API, SMTP relay, and email marketing campaigns for developers and businesses sending high volumes of email.
enrichment.pricing_model: Usage-based subscription tiers for the Email API, plus separate Marketing Campaigns plans
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan sends up to 100 emails per day; paid Email API plans start around $19.95 per month, and Marketing Campaigns paid plans start at $15 per month
enrichment.company_size: Operated as part of Twilio, a public company (NYSE: TWLO) with roughly 5,500-plus employees company-wide
enrichment.headquarters: Originally founded in Denver, Colorado; now operated as a Twilio product, with Twilio headquartered in San Francisco, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: SendGrid (Twilio SendGrid) Review 2026: Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See Twilio SendGrid's Email API and Marketing Campaigns pricing, deliverability features, and company background for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SendGrid is a cloud-based email delivery platform founded in 2009 in Denver, Colorado by Isaac Saldana, Jose Lopez, and Tim Jenkins, and incubated through the Techstars accelerator program before raising early funding from Highway 12 Ventures. The company grew into one of the largest transactional email API providers before being acquired by Twilio in 2019 for approximately 2 billion dollars, after which it was rebranded as Twilio SendGrid and folded into Twilio's broader communications platform, which is headquartered in San Francisco, California and trades publicly on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker TWLO with several thousand employees company-wide. Today SendGrid operates as two related but separately priced product lines under the Twilio umbrella: the Email API, aimed at developers who need to send transactional email such as password resets, receipts, and notifications through a RESTful API or SMTP relay, and Marketing Campaigns, aimed at marketers sending newsletters and promotional email with drag-and-drop design tools and audience segmentation. The Email API offers a limited free trial tier of up to 100 emails per day, with paid Essentials plans generally ranging from about $19.95 to $29.95 per month and Pro plans ranging from about $89.95 to $949.95 per month depending on sending volume, dedicated IP needs, and sub-user management requirements. Marketing Campaigns has its own free tier covering up to 2,000 contacts and 6,000 emails per month, with paid plans starting at $15 per month and scaling with contact list size and automation needs. Key platform features include dynamic email templates with both drag-and-drop and code-based editors, an email validation API to reduce bounce rates, deliverability tooling such as dedicated IP addresses, IP warmup schedules, and domain authentication (SPF, DKIM, and DMARC support), real-time analytics with event webhooks for tracking opens, clicks, and bounces, and integration with the wider Twilio ecosystem so businesses can combine email with SMS, voice, and other communication channels in unified customer workflows. SendGrid's infrastructure is built for high-volume, high-reliability sending, with the platform collectively delivering tens of billions of emails per month across its customer base, making it a common choice for SaaS companies and developers who need dependable, API-first transactional email at scale, though its marketing automation depth is generally considered less advanced than purpose-built email service providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SendGrid was founded in 2009 in Denver, Colorado, and grew into a major transactional email provider before Twilio acquired it in 2019 for approximately 2 billion dollars. It now operates as Twilio SendGrid, part of the publicly traded Twilio communications platform headquartered in San Francisco.

The platform is split into two product lines: the developer-focused Email API for transactional email, and Marketing Campaigns for newsletters and promotional email, each with its own pricing structure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SendGrid's Email API supports RESTful and SMTP integration, dynamic templates with drag-and-drop and code editors, an email validation API to reduce bounces, and deliverability tools including dedicated IPs, IP warmup, and domain authentication.

Marketing Campaigns adds newsletter design, automation, and segmentation tools, while real-time analytics and event webhooks give visibility into opens, clicks, and bounces across both product lines, and integration with the broader Twilio ecosystem allows combined email, SMS, and voice workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Email API offers a limited free trial of up to 100 emails per day, with paid Essentials plans around $19.95 to $29.95 per month and Pro plans from $89.95 to $949.95 per month depending on volume and dedicated IP needs.
Marketing Campaigns has a separate free tier for up to 2,000 contacts and 6,000 emails per month, with paid plans starting at $15 per month and scaling with list size and automation requirements.

features.description: Combine email with SMS, voice, and other Twilio communication channels in unified workflows.

features.description: Built to reliably deliver tens of billions of emails per month across its customer base.

Robust, well-documented developer API
Strong deliverability infrastructure including dedicated IPs and domain authentication
Part of the broader Twilio communications platform for combined channel workflows
Free tier available for testing and low-volume use
cons: Pricing can escalate quickly at high sending volumes
Marketing automation features are less advanced than dedicated ESPs

Separate pricing for Email API versus Marketing Campaigns can be confusing to compare

faqs.question: Is SendGrid the same as Twilio?
faqs.answer: SendGrid was acquired by Twilio in 2019 and now operates as Twilio SendGrid, a product line within the broader Twilio communications platform.

faqs.question: How much does SendGrid cost?
faqs.answer: The Email API has paid plans generally starting around $19.95 per month, while Marketing Campaigns paid plans start at $15 per month, both scaling with volume.

faqs.question: Does SendGrid have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a limited free trial tier sending up to 100 emails per day for the Email API, and a free Marketing Campaigns tier covering up to 2,000 contacts.

faqs.question: What is SendGrid mainly used for?
faqs.answer: SendGrid is primarily used for transactional email such as password resets and receipts, as well as marketing newsletters through its separate Marketing Campaigns product.

faqs.question: When was SendGrid founded?
faqs.answer: SendGrid was founded in 2009 in Denver, Colorado by Isaac Saldana, Jose Lopez, and Tim Jenkins.

faqs.question: Does SendGrid support dedicated IP addresses?
faqs.answer: Yes, dedicated IPs are available on higher-tier Email API plans to help manage sender reputation.

faqs.question: Can SendGrid integrate with other Twilio products?
faqs.answer: Yes, because it is part of Twilio, SendGrid can be combined with Twilio's SMS, voice, and other communication APIs in unified workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Sendible is a social media management platform for agencies and marketing teams to schedule, publish, monitor, and report on content across Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, X, YouTube, Google Business Profile, Threads, and Bluesky from one dashboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription, billed monthly or annually
enrichment.starting_price: Creator plan starts at $29 per month for one user and six social profiles
enrichment.company_size: 50 to 100 employees; bootstrapped, acquired by Traject in 2021
enrichment.seo_title: Sendible Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Plans for Agencies
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Sendible's pricing tiers, supported social platforms, and agency-focused features for social media management in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sendible is a social media management platform founded in 2008 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, built primarily for social media agencies and marketing teams that manage multiple client accounts. The company is bootstrapped rather than venture-backed, employs an estimated 50 to 100 people, and was acquired by Traject, a portfolio company that also owns other marketing software brands, in March 2021, though it continues to operate under the Sendible name. The platform allows users to schedule, publish, monitor, and report on content across a wide set of networks, including Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, X (formerly Twitter), YouTube, Google Business Profile, Threads, and Bluesky, all from a single unified dashboard, which is notable for including newer platforms like Threads and Bluesky alongside established networks. Key features include a bulk content importer that lets users schedule a month's worth of posts at once, Smart Queues that automatically recycle evergreen content on a recurring schedule, a unified social inbox for managing comments, direct messages, and mentions across platforms without switching accounts, a built-in image editor with integrations for GIPHY, Pexels, and Canva, white-label and automated performance reporting aimed at agencies presenting results to clients, and content approval workflows that support team and client collaboration before posts go live. Sendible's pricing is structured across four tiers: Creator at $29 per month for one user and six social profiles, aimed at freelance social media managers; Traction at $89 per month, adding more users, profiles, and reporting; Advanced ranging from roughly $299 to $524 per month for larger teams needing collaboration and approval workflows; and Enterprise ranging from roughly $750 to $891 per month for the largest profile and user allowances alongside white-label reporting. This makes Sendible notably more expensive at its top tiers than entry-level competitors, though its agency-oriented features such as white-label reports and client approval workflows are specifically designed to justify that cost for agencies managing many client accounts. Because Sendible has been bootstrapped and independently operated since 2008 rather than aggressively venture-funded, it has built a long operating history and stable feature set, though some reviewers note the interface can feel less modern than newer social media scheduling competitors such as Buffer, Hootsuite, or Later, and that some advanced analytics remain gated behind the higher-priced plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sendible is a social media management platform founded in 2008 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom. The company is bootstrapped, employs an estimated 50 to 100 people, and was acquired by Traject in March 2021 while continuing to operate under its own brand.

The platform is built primarily for agencies and marketing teams managing multiple client social accounts, offering scheduling, publishing, monitoring, and reporting across a wide range of social networks from a single dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sendible supports scheduling across Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok, X, YouTube, Google Business Profile, Threads, and Bluesky, with a bulk content importer, Smart Queues for recycling evergreen content, and a unified social inbox for comments, DMs, and mentions.

Agency-oriented features include a built-in image editor with Canva, GIPHY, and Pexels integration, white-label and automated performance reports, and content approval workflows supporting team and client collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sendible's pricing starts at $29 per month for the Creator plan, covering one user and six social profiles, and moves up to $89 per month for Traction with additional users and profiles.
Larger teams pay $299 to $524 per month for the Advanced tier, and agencies needing the highest profile and user counts along with white-label reporting pay $750 to $891 per month for Enterprise.

enrichment.short_description: Sendmail is one of the oldest mail transfer agents (MTA) on the internet, originally created at UC Berkeley in the early 1980s, now maintained as an open-source project and Sentrion-based commercial offering under Proofpoint.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free open-source MTA, with commercial Sentrion-based support and enterprise features available through Proofpoint
enrichment.starting_price: Free as an open-source distribution; commercial and enterprise pricing available on request from Proofpoint
enrichment.company_size: Sendmail, Inc. was a small company acquired for about 23 million dollars in 2013; it now operates under Proofpoint, which has approximately 5,000 employees
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Emeryville, California; now maintained under Proofpoint, headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Sendmail Guide 2026: History, Current Status, and Proofpoint Ownership
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn Sendmail's origin as a pioneering open-source mail transfer agent, its Proofpoint ownership, and current commercial and open-source options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sendmail is one of the earliest mail transfer agents (MTA) on the internet, originally written as a derivative of an earlier program called delivermail by Eric Allman at the University of California, Berkeley, and first shipped with the BSD 4.1c operating system in 1983, making it one of the very first mail servers to support TCP/IP-based email delivery. Sendmail became the dominant mail server software of the early internet era, with an estimated 80 percent of publicly reachable mail servers running it in 1996, though its market share declined sharply from the mid-2000s onward as competitors Postfix and Exim gained adoption, with Exim in particular capturing much of the web hosting market through its bundling with cPanel. The commercial entity behind the software, Sendmail, Inc., was founded in 1999 by Eric Allman and was based in Emeryville, California, later building a commercial product line called Sentrion for enterprise email infrastructure on top of the open-source core. In October 2013, Sendmail, Inc. was acquired by Proofpoint, Inc. for approximately 23 million dollars in cash, and Proofpoint has since maintained both the free open-source Sendmail distribution for the community and a commercial Sentrion-based offering for enterprise customers needing dedicated support. Proofpoint itself was later taken private in 2021 when it was acquired by the private equity firm Thoma Bravo for approximately 12.3 billion dollars, and today Proofpoint is headquartered in Sunnyvale, California with roughly 5,000 employees globally. As of 2026, the open-source Sendmail project continues to receive periodic maintenance releases, with recent versions such as 8.18.2 signed under current-year signing keys, indicating the codebase remains actively maintained rather than abandoned, even though its real-world usage share has fallen to a small fraction of internet mail servers compared to its 1990s peak. Sendmail's configuration is defined through the notoriously complex sendmail.cf syntax, which offers extremely fine-grained control over mail routing, address rewriting, and multi-domain relay scenarios, but is widely regarded as harder to learn than the configuration approaches used by Postfix or Exim. Sendmail supports extensive third-party milter (mail filter) integrations for spam and virus scanning, and remains present in some long-running enterprise and legacy Unix environments where it was deployed decades ago and has not been migrated away from since.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sendmail originated in the early 1980s at UC Berkeley as a derivative of an earlier program called delivermail, and shipped with BSD 4.1c in 1983 as one of the first mail servers to support TCP/IP email delivery. It dominated internet mail routing through the 1990s before losing significant market share to Postfix and Exim in the 2000s.

The commercial company behind it, Sendmail, Inc., was founded in 1999 by creator Eric Allman and was acquired by Proofpoint in October 2013 for about 23 million dollars. Proofpoint, now headquartered in Sunnyvale, California, was itself taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2021 and continues to maintain both the open-source Sendmail project and a commercial Sentrion-based offering.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sendmail is a highly configurable, battle-tested MTA that handles SMTP mail routing, address rewriting, and complex multi-domain relay scenarios through its sendmail.cf configuration language, refined over more than 40 years of production use.

It supports extensive third-party milter integrations for spam and virus filtering, and its commercial Sentrion platform builds enterprise features such as dedicated support and advanced routing controls on top of the open-source core.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The open-source Sendmail distribution is free to download and self-support, maintained by Proofpoint for the community.

Organizations needing dedicated support or the Sentrion-based enterprise platform must contact Proofpoint directly for custom commercial pricing, as no public list pricing is available for the enterprise offering.

features.title: Battle-tested SMTP mail routing
features.description: Decades of production use handling core SMTP mail transfer and routing on the internet.

features.title: Rule-based configuration via sendmail.cf
features.description: Highly configurable mail routing and address rewriting through a mature, if complex, configuration language.

features.title: Open-source distribution
features.description: Maintained by Proofpoint as a free, community-available open-source project.

features.title: Sentrion commercial platform
features.description: Proofpoint's commercial enterprise email infrastructure platform built on Sendmail technology.

features.title: Broad Unix and Linux compatibility
features.description: Historically shipped with many BSD and Unix-derived operating systems.

features.title: Milter support
features.description: Extensive third-party mail filter (milter) integrations for spam and virus scanning.

features.title: Multi-domain relay routing
features.description: Supports complex multi-domain, multi-relay enterprise mail routing scenarios.

features.title: Mature documentation base
features.description: Decades of accumulated documentation and community knowledge from its long operating history.

pros: Extremely mature and battle-tested, powering internet mail since the early 1980s
Free and open source with an actively maintained community distribution
Highly configurable for complex, non-standard mail routing needs
Large historical body of documentation and community knowledge
Backed by Proofpoint's enterprise support for commercial Sentrion deployments
cons: The sendmail.cf configuration syntax is notoriously difficult to learn
Largely superseded in new deployments by Postfix and Exim
Smaller active community than modern MTA alternatives
Fewer modern built-in security features compared to newer mail servers
Market share has declined sharply since its 1990s peak of around 80 percent

faqs.question: What is Sendmail?
faqs.answer: Sendmail is one of the oldest mail transfer agents (MTA) on the internet, originally created in the early 1980s at UC Berkeley to route SMTP email between servers.

faqs.question: Is Sendmail still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, the open-source Sendmail project continues to receive periodic maintenance releases under Proofpoint's stewardship as of 2026.

faqs.question: Who owns Sendmail now?
faqs.answer: Sendmail, Inc. was acquired by Proofpoint in October 2013, and Proofpoint was later taken private by Thoma Bravo in 2021.

faqs.question: Is Sendmail free?
faqs.answer: The core Sendmail distribution is free and open source; Proofpoint's Sentrion-based commercial platform and enterprise support are separately priced.

faqs.question: How does Sendmail compare to Postfix and Exim?
faqs.answer: Sendmail is older and more established but has a harder-to-learn configuration syntax, and has lost significant market share to Postfix and Exim in new deployments since the mid-2000s.

faqs.question: How old is Sendmail?
faqs.answer: Sendmail originated in the early 1980s, first shipping with BSD 4.1c in 1983, making it roughly four decades old as of 2026.

enrichment.short_description: SendPulse is a multi-channel marketing automation platform combining email, SMS, web push notifications, and chatbots for WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram, and Facebook, along with a free CRM, landing page builder, and course and LMS tools.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription with pay-as-you-go and per-channel add-on options
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan includes up to 15,000 emails per month; paid plans start around $9.85 per month
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States, with additional offices in Ukraine, Brazil, and Mexico
enrichment.seo_title: SendPulse Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Free Plan
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore SendPulse's email, SMS, chatbot, and CRM features and 2026 pricing tiers, including its free plan and pay-as-you-go option.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SendPulse is a multi-channel marketing automation platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in New York, New York, with additional operational offices in Ukraine, Brazil, and Mexico reflecting its globally distributed team of roughly 159 employees. The platform has grown to reach around 3 million registered users as of mid-2025, with more than 1,000 new signups reported daily, and it reports around 5.8 million dollars in annual recurring revenue. SendPulse differentiates itself from single-channel email tools by bundling several marketing channels into one platform: email marketing with a drag-and-drop builder and automation flows, SMS marketing, web push notifications, and chatbot builders for WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram, and Facebook Messenger, all manageable from a single interface. Beyond messaging channels, the platform includes a free built-in CRM for managing sales pipelines and deals, a landing page and website builder with customizable templates, multi-step automation workflows that can chain together email, SMS, and chatbot triggers based on subscriber behavior, transactional email sending via SMTP or API, and an online course and learning management system (LMS) builder for creators selling digital content, making SendPulse considerably broader in scope than a typical email service provider. Pricing follows a freemium model: the free plan covers up to 15,000 emails per month along with basic CRM and automation access, while paid Standard plans start around $9.85 per month and scale based on subscriber count, with higher Pro tiers unlocking more advanced automation and A/B testing. SendPulse also offers a pay-as-you-go option starting at roughly 32 dollars for 10,000 emails for businesses that prefer not to commit to a recurring subscription, and a custom-priced VIP or Enterprise tier for organizations with subscriber lists exceeding one million contacts. Because SendPulse spans so many products, including CRM, chatbots, landing pages, and an LMS, in addition to core email and SMS marketing, it trades some depth for breadth compared to specialized single-channel tools, and some advanced automation and reporting features are reserved for higher-tier paid plans, but its combination of a genuinely useful free tier, competitive pay-as-you-go email pricing, and multi-channel messaging including WhatsApp and Telegram chatbots makes it a distinctive option for small and mid-sized businesses that want to consolidate several marketing tools into one subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SendPulse is a multi-channel marketing automation platform founded in 2015, headquartered in New York, with additional offices in Ukraine, Brazil, and Mexico supporting its globally distributed team of roughly 159 employees.

The platform has grown to around 3 million registered users as of mid-2025 by bundling email, SMS, web push, and chatbot messaging alongside a free CRM, landing page builder, and course and LMS tools into a single subscription.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core channels include email marketing with a drag-and-drop builder, SMS marketing, web push notifications, and chatbot builders for WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram, and Facebook Messenger, all manageable from one dashboard.

Additional tools include a free built-in CRM for pipeline and deal management, a landing page and website builder, multi-step automation that chains email, SMS, and chatbot triggers together, and an online course and LMS builder for selling digital content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SendPulse's free plan covers up to 15,000 emails per month with basic CRM and automation access, while paid Standard plans start around $9.85 per month and scale with subscriber count.

A pay-as-you-go option starts at roughly 32 dollars for 10,000 emails for businesses avoiding a recurring subscription, and a custom-priced VIP or Enterprise tier is available for lists exceeding one million subscribers.

enrichment.short_description: Sendy is a self-hosted email newsletter application that runs on your own server and sends bulk campaigns through Amazon SES, offering a one-time license fee instead of a recurring monthly subscription.
enrichment.pricing_model: One-time license purchase for self-hosted software, with no recurring subscription fee
enrichment.starting_price: $69 one-time payment for a single-site license
enrichment.seo_title: Sendy Review 2026: One-Time Pricing and Amazon SES Setup
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Sendy's self-hosted, one-time-fee email newsletter software works with Amazon SES, including pricing and core features in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sendy is a self-hosted email newsletter application that a small independent development team built to run on standard PHP and MySQL or MariaDB web hosting, distinguishing itself from mainstream email service providers through a one-time license fee model rather than a recurring monthly subscription. Instead of paying a per-subscriber monthly fee to a SaaS provider, Sendy customers pay a single one-time license fee of $69 to install and run the software on their own server, giving them full ownership of their sending infrastructure and subscriber data. Sendy sends campaigns through Amazon Simple Email Service (Amazon SES), Amazon's cloud-based transactional and bulk email delivery service, which allows Sendy users to send bulk email at a cost of roughly $1 per 10,000 emails, a small fraction of what typical subscription-based email marketing platforms charge for equivalent sending volume. This combination of a one-time software fee plus extremely low-cost Amazon SES sending is Sendy's core value proposition and has made it a popular choice among publishers, bloggers, and developers who send large newsletters regularly and want to minimize ongoing costs. Core features include a drag-and-drop email builder for creating campaigns without writing code, subscriber list management with support for custom fields and audience segments, campaign analytics that track opens, clicks, bounces, and unsubscribes, basic autoresponder and automation workflows, and an API that lets developers integrate external signup forms and applications directly with Sendy's subscriber lists. Because Sendy is self-hosted, users are responsible for installing and maintaining the application on their own server, configuring their own Amazon SES account and sending domain, and managing their own sender reputation and deliverability, which requires more technical comfort than a typical SaaS email tool but also removes the platform's control over sending limits, subscriber caps, or price increases over time. Publicly available sources do not provide a clearly verifiable founding year, headquarters location, or company size for Sendy, and unlike most SaaS competitors, Sendy does not publish detailed corporate information, consistent with its positioning as a lightweight, developer-oriented software product rather than a large marketing organization; prospective buyers evaluating it should weigh its low fixed cost and full data ownership against the added responsibility of self-hosting, maintaining server security and updates, and managing SES deliverability without a dedicated support team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sendy is a self-hosted email newsletter application built on PHP and MySQL or MariaDB, designed to run on standard web hosting rather than as a hosted SaaS product. Its defining feature is a one-time license fee rather than a recurring monthly subscription.

Sendy sends campaigns through Amazon SES, which allows for bulk email delivery at roughly $1 per 10,000 emails, making it especially popular with publishers and developers who send high volumes of newsletters and want to minimize ongoing sending costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include a drag-and-drop email builder, subscriber list management with custom fields and segments, campaign analytics covering opens, clicks, bounces, and unsubscribes, and basic autoresponder and automation workflows.

An API is available for integrating external signup forms and applications directly with Sendy's subscriber lists, and because the software is self-hosted, users retain full control and ownership over their subscriber data and sending infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sendy is sold as a single-site license for a one-time payment of $69, which unlocks unlimited campaigns and subscribers on that installation with no recurring subscription fee.
Ongoing costs are limited to Amazon SES usage, priced at roughly $1 per 10,000 emails sent, plus whatever web hosting the user already pays for to run the self-hosted application.

Deliverability and Amazon SES reputation management is the user's own responsibility
Fewer built-in integrations than mainstream SaaS email service providers
Interface and templates are more basic than modern hosted competitors

faqs.question: What is Sendy?
faqs.answer: Sendy is a self-hosted email newsletter application that runs on your own server and sends bulk email through Amazon SES.

faqs.question: How much does Sendy cost?
faqs.answer: Sendy uses a one-time license fee of $69 per site, rather than a recurring monthly subscription.

faqs.question: Does Sendy require Amazon SES?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sendy is built to send campaigns through Amazon SES, which typically costs about $1 per 10,000 emails sent.

faqs.question: Is Sendy self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sendy must be installed on your own PHP and MySQL or MariaDB web server rather than being used as a hosted SaaS product.

faqs.question: Is Sendy good for large newsletters?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sendy is popular with publishers and bloggers sending large newsletters because Amazon SES sending costs are much lower than typical subscription-based email platforms.

faqs.question: Does Sendy have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sendy provides an API for integrating external signup forms and applications with its subscriber lists.

faqs.question: Who maintains Sendy?
faqs.answer: Sendy is maintained by a small independent development team; detailed public information about company size, founding date, and headquarters is not published.

use_cases.title: High-volume newsletter publishing
use_cases.description: Bloggers and publishers with large subscriber lists use Sendy to cut recurring sending costs via Amazon SES.

use_cases.title: Developer-controlled email infrastructure
use_cases.description: Developers and agencies use Sendy for full control over their email sending stack instead of relying on a third-party SaaS provider.

use_cases.title: Cost-sensitive bulk sending
use_cases.description: Businesses with very large lists use Sendy to minimize per-email sending costs compared to subscription-based ESPs.

features: Unlimited campaigns and subscribers on one installation
Billed directly through Amazon Web Services
